'''Hanksy''' is an anonymous [[graffiti artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title = Meet Hanksy, the Pun-Loving Graffiti Trickster With a Serious Cult Following|url = http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|accessdate = 2015-08-29|website = [[Rolling Stone]]}}</ref> As the name implies, he is most noted for his parodies in which he combines pieces from British graffiti artist [[Banksy]] with the face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title = A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|url = http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|newspaper = [[The New York Times]]|date = 2014-02-14|access-date = 2015-08-29|issn = 0362-4331|first = John|last = Leland}}</ref>

His career began in April 2011, when he created a cut-out of a rat holding a paint roller with an image of Hank's face over it. He then pasted it on a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] and posted a picture of it on [[Instagram]]. It eventually went [[Viral phenomenon|viral]] and by 2014 Hanksy was collecting thousands for the Banksy-Hanks mashups at sold out shows across the country.<ref name=":0" />

Hanksy has been recognized for other works and shows beyond his signature parody. In December 2013 he held a collaborative "building destruction party" in an abandoned [[East Village, Manhattan|East Village]] tenement, filling the walls with art. The show was called ''Surplus Candy'', and involved over twenty local street artists.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Photos, Video: Hanksy Hosted An Unauthorized Art Show Inside Abandoned East Village Tenement|url = http://gothamist.com/2014/01/11/photos_inside_an_abandoned_east_vil.php|website = Gothamist|accessdate = 2015-08-29|first = Jen|last = Carlson}}</ref>

It eventually went [[Viral phenomenon|viral]] and by 2014 Hanksy was collecting over  for his mashups at sold out shows across the country.<ref name=":0" /> His work has branched out to parody other celebrities, such as [[Ted Danson]], [[Ryan Gosling]] and [[Will Smith]].<ref>{{Cite news|title = Hanksy, Milbanksy: What will the next Banksy parody be?|url = https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/style-blog/post/hanksy-milbanksy-what-will-the-next-banksy-parody-be/2012/02/21/gIQA8qrnTR_blog.html|newspaper = The Washington Post|date = 2012-02-21|access-date = 2015-08-29|issn = 0190-8286|language = en-US|first = Maura|last = Judkis}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title = Hanksy|url = http://www.hanksy.com/|website = Hanksy (official site)|accessdate = 2015-08-29}}</ref>

Hanksy has been recognized for both his microbe is and  other works and shows beyond his signature parodies. In December 2013 he held collaborative "building destruction party" in an abandoned [[East Village, Manhattan|East Village]] tenement, filling the walls with art. The show was called ''Surplus Candy'', and involved over twenty local street artists.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Photos, Video: Hanksy Hosted An Unauthorized Art Show Inside Abandoned East Village Tenement|url = http://gothamist.com/2014/01/11/photos_inside_an_abandoned_east_vil.php|website = [[Gothamist]]|accessdate = 2015-08-29|first = Jen|last = Carlson}}</ref>

Hanksy curated over 90 artists painting the inside of an abandoned 100-year-old mansion. <ref>http://laist.com/2015/10/12/hanksy_surplus_candy_show.php</ref>

In September 2015 he painted a large scale mural of Republican candidate Donald Trump depicted as a giant piece of feces. <ref>http://www.nydailynews.com/new-york/hanksy-paints-mural-donald-trump-giant-pile-s-article-1.2340583</ref>

'''Hanksy''' is an anonymous [[graffiti artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title = Meet Hanksy, the Pun-Loving Graffiti Trickster With a Serious Cult Following|url = http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|accessdate = 2015-08-29|website = [[Rolling Stone]]}}</ref> As the name implies, he is most noted for his parodies in which he combines pieces from British graffiti artist [[Banksy]] with the face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title = A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|url = http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|newspaper = [[The New York Times]]|date = 2014-02-14|access-date = 2015-08-29|issn = 0362-4331|first = John|last = Leland}}</ref>

His career began in April 2011, when he created a cut-out of a Banksy rat holding a paint roller with an image of Hanks' face over it. He then pasted it on a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] and someone posted a picture of it on [[Instagram]]. It eventually went [[Viral phenomenon|viral]] and by 2014 Hanksy was collecting thousands for his mashups at sold out shows across the country.<ref name=":0" /> The artist's exhibitions seem to be hyper-transitory spectacles, attracting thousands in just a few short hours. Since his inception, his work has "branched out" by adding other wordplay and celebrities, such as [[Ted Danson]], [[Ryan Gosling]] and [[Will Smith]].<ref>{{Cite news|title = Hanksy, Milbanksy: What will the next Banksy parody be?|url = https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/style-blog/post/hanksy-milbanksy-what-will-the-next-banksy-parody-be/2012/02/21/gIQA8qrnTR_blog.html|newspaper = The Washington Post|date = 2012-02-21|access-date = 2015-08-29|issn = 0190-8286|language = en-US|first = Maura|last = Judkis}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title = Hanksy|url = http://www.hanksy.com/|website = Hanksy (official site)|accessdate = 2015-08-29}}</ref>

Hanksy only accepts payment for his art in the form of chili-cheese fritos.

Though his origins are unknown it's highly documented that Hanksy needs to do more in the city of Milwaukee cause of beer and fritos.

Hanksy has been recognized for works beyond his signature parodies. In December 2013 he held a collaborative "building destruction party" in an abandoned [[East Village, Manhattan|East Village]] tenement, filling the walls with art. The show was called ''Surplus Candy'', and involved over twenty local street artists.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Photos, Video: Hanksy Hosted An Unauthorized Art Show Inside Abandoned East Village Tenement|url = http://gothamist.com/2014/01/11/photos_inside_an_abandoned_east_vil.php|website = [[Gothamist]]|accessdate = 2015-08-29|first = Jen|last = Carlson}}</ref> He followed up this event with a West Coast edition in late 2015. Hanksy curated over 90 artists painting the inside of an abandoned 100-year-old mansion. <ref>http://laist.com/2015/10/12/hanksy_surplus_candy_show.php</ref>

In September 2015 he painted a large scale mural of Republican candidate Donald Trump depicted as a giant piece of feces. <ref>http://www.nydailynews.com/new-york/hanksy-paints-mural-donald-trump-giant-pile-s-article-1.2340583</ref>

Surplus Candy is also known for huge piles of chocolate left by Hanksy fans worshipping the art. Since then Hershey has released the Hanksy Bar so that fans had a candy to give him at all Surplus Candy stores. He also accepts Fritos as offerings

His career began in April 2011, when he created a cut-out of a Banksy rat holding a paint roller with an image of Hank's face over it. He then pasted it on a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] and posted a picture of it on [[Instagram]]. It eventually went [[Viral phenomenon|viral]] and by 2014 Hanksy was collecting thousands for the Banksy-Hanks mashups at sold out shows across the country.<ref name=":0" /> His work has branched out to parody other celebrities, such as [[Ted Danson]], [[Ryan Gosling]] and [[Will Smith]].<ref>{{Cite news|title = Hanksy, Milbanksy: What will the next Banksy parody be?|url = https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/style-blog/post/hanksy-milbanksy-what-will-the-next-banksy-parody-be/2012/02/21/gIQA8qrnTR_blog.html|newspaper = The Washington Post|date = 2012-02-21|access-date = 2015-08-29|issn = 0190-8286|language = en-US|first = Maura|last = Judkis}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title = Hanksy|url = http://www.hanksy.com/|website = Hanksy (official site)|accessdate = 2015-08-29}}</ref>

Hanksy has been recognized for other works and shows beyond his signature parodies. In December 2013 he held a collaborative "building destruction party" in an abandoned [[East Village, Manhattan|East Village]] tenement, filling the walls with art. The show was called ''Surplus Candy'', and involved over twenty local street artists.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Photos, Video: Hanksy Hosted An Unauthorized Art Show Inside Abandoned East Village Tenement|url = http://gothamist.com/2014/01/11/photos_inside_an_abandoned_east_vil.php|website = [[Gothamist]]|accessdate = 2015-08-29|first = Jen|last = Carlson}}</ref>

{{Orphan|date=August 2015}}

'''Hanksy''' is an anonymous [[graffiti artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref>{{Cite web|title = Meet Hanksy, the Pun-Loving Graffiti Trickster With a Serious Cult Following|url = http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|accessdate = 2015-08-29|website = [[Rolling Stone]]}}</ref> As the name implies, he is most noted for his parodies in which he combines pieces from British graffiti artist [[Banksy]] with the face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|title = A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|url = http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|newspaper = [[The New York Times]]|date = 2014-02-14|access-date = 2015-08-29|issn = 0362-4331|first = John|last = Leland}}</ref>

His career began in April 2011, when he created a cut-out of a Banksy rat holding a paint roller with an image of Hank's face over it. He then pasted it on a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] and someone posted a picture of it on [[Instagram]]. It eventually went [[Viral phenomenon|viral]] and by 2014 Hanksy was collecting thousands for the Banksy-Hanks mashups at sold out shows across the country.<ref name=":0" /> His work has since branched out, including other celebrities and light-hearted imagery - most notably, a giant mural of Presidential candidate Donald Trump depicted as a large pile of excrement. <ref>http://www.nydailynews.com/new-york/hanksy-paints-mural-donald-trump-giant-pile-s-article-1.2340583</ref><ref>{{Cite news|title = Hanksy, Milbanksy: What will the next Banksy parody be?|url = https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/style-blog/post/hanksy-milbanksy-what-will-the-next-banksy-parody-be/2012/02/21/gIQA8qrnTR_blog.html|newspaper = The Washington Post|date = 2012-02-21|access-date = 2015-08-29|issn = 0190-8286|language = en|first = Maura|last = Judkis}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title = Hanksy|url = http://www.hanksy.com/|website = Hanksy (official site)|accessdate = 2015-08-29}}</ref>

Hanksy has been recognized for other works and shows beyond his signature parodies. n December 2013 he held a collaborative "building destruction party" in an abandoned [[East Village, Manhattan|East Village]] tenement, filling the walls with art. The show was called ''Surplus Candy'', and involved over twenty local street artists.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Photos, Video: Hanksy Hosted An Unauthorized Art Show Inside Abandoned East Village Tenement|url = http://gothamist.com/2014/01/11/photos_inside_an_abandoned_east_vil.php|website = [[Gothamist]]|accessdate = 2015-08-29|first = Jen|last = Carlson}}</ref> A similar, but larger event took place in Los Angeles during October 2015. ''Surplus Candy LA'' was open for one night only in a dilapidated LA mansion. Over 90 artists were involved with attendance reaching beyond 2,000 people. <ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.lamag.com/culturefiles/street-artist-hanksy-talks-fairy-dust-grilled-lamb-and-graffiti/|title=Street Artist Hanksy Talks Fairy Dust, Grilled Lamb, and Graffiti|author=Jessica P. Ogilvie|date=10 October 2015|work=Los Angeles Magazine|accessdate=3 February 2016}}</ref>

[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]]
In August 2015, two months after [[Donald Trump]] announced he would [[Donald Trump presidential campaign, 2016|run for President of the Untied States]], Hanksy painted a mural the ''[[Huffington Post]]'' dubbed ''Tronald Dump'', depicting the candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces.<ref name=huffpo>{{cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.co.uk/2015/08/28/donald-trump-poop-poo-turd-new-york-city-graffiti-painting-mural_n_8055210.html|title=Painting Of Donald Trump As A Pile Of Poop By Street Artist 'Hanksy' Appears In Manhattan, New York|last=Barrell|first=Ryan|date=28 August 2015|work=Huffington Post}}</ref><ref name=dailynews>{{cite web|work=Daily News|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/new-york/hanksy-paints-mural-donald-trump-giant-pile-s-article-1.2340583|title=NYC street artist Hanksy paints mural of Donald Trump as 'a giant pile of s---'|date=28 August 2015|last=Katz|first=Celeste}}</ref><ref name=gothamist>{{cite web|work=Gothamist|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/08/28/trumpe_loeil.php|title=Photo: Hanksy Paints Stunningly Lifelike Portrait Of Donald Trump|last=Yakas|first=Ben|date=28 August 2015}}</ref><ref name=cbs>{{cite web|work=CBS New York|url=http://newyork.cbslocal.com/2015/08/28/donald-trump-poop-mural-hansky/|title=NYC Street Artist Paints Mural Of Donald Trump As ‘Giant Pile Of S—‘|date=28 August 2015|last=Jones|first=Al}}</ref><ref name=artnet>{{cite web|url=https://news.artnet.com/art-world/hanksy-donald-trump-shit-329090|title=Hanksy Painted a Mural of Donald Trump as a Pile of Poop|last=Boucher|first=Brian|date=31 August 2015|work=Artnet}}</ref>

He then pasted it on a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] and someone posted a picture of it on [[Instagram]]. It eventually went [[Viral phenomenon|viral]] and by 2014 Hanksy was collecting thousands for the Banksy-Hanks mashups at sold out shows across the country.<ref name=":0" /> His work has since branched out, including other celebrities and light-hearted imagery - most notably, a giant mural of Presidential candidate Donald Trump depicted as a large pile of excrement. <ref>http://www.nydailynews.com/new-york/hanksy-paints-mural-donald-trump-giant-pile-s-article-1.2340583</ref><ref>{{Cite news|title = Hanksy, Milbanksy: What will the next Banksy parody be?|url = https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/style-blog/post/hanksy-milbanksy-what-will-the-next-banksy-parody-be/2012/02/21/gIQA8qrnTR_blog.html|newspaper = The Washington Post|date = 2012-02-21|access-date = 2015-08-29|issn = 0190-8286|language = en|first = Maura|last = Judkis}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title = Hanksy|url = http://www.hanksy.com/|website = Hanksy (official site)|accessdate = 2015-08-29}}</ref>

Hanksy has been recognized for other works and shows beyond his signature parodies. n December 2013 he held a collaborative "building destruction party" in an abandoned [[East Village, Manhattan|East Village]] tenement, filling the walls with art. Over 90 artists were involved with attendance reaching beyond 2,000 people. <ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.lamag.com/culturefiles/street-artist-hanksy-talks-fairy-dust-grilled-lamb-and-graffiti/|title=Street Artist Hanksy Talks Fairy Dust, Grilled Lamb, and Graffiti|author=Jessica P. Ogilvie|date=10 October 2015|work=Los Angeles Magazine|accessdate=3 February 2016}}</ref>

[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]]
In August 2015, two months after [[Donald Trump]] announced he would [[Donald Trump presidential campaign, 2016|run for President of the United States]], Hanksy painted a mural the ''[[Huffington Post]]'' dubbed ''Tronald Dump'', depicting the candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces.<ref name=huffpo>{{cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.co.uk/2015/08/28/donald-trump-poop-poo-turd-new-york-city-graffiti-painting-mural_n_8055210.html|title=Painting Of Donald Trump As A Pile Of Poop By Street Artist 'Hanksy' Appears In Manhattan, New York|last=Barrell|first=Ryan|date=28 August 2015|work=Huffington Post}}</ref><ref name=dailynews>{{cite web|work=Daily News|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/new-york/hanksy-paints-mural-donald-trump-giant-pile-s-article-1.2340583|title=NYC street artist Hanksy paints mural of Donald Trump as 'a giant pile of s---'|date=28 August 2015|last=Katz|first=Celeste}}</ref><ref name=gothamist>{{cite web|work=Gothamist|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/08/28/trumpe_loeil.php|title=Photo: Hanksy Paints Stunningly Lifelike Portrait Of Donald Trump|last=Yakas|first=Ben|date=28 August 2015}}</ref><ref name=cbs>{{cite web|work=CBS New York|url=http://newyork.cbslocal.com/2015/08/28/donald-trump-poop-mural-hansky/|title=NYC Street Artist Paints Mural Of Donald Trump As ‘Giant Pile Of S—‘|date=28 August 2015|last=Jones|first=Al}}</ref><ref name=artnet>{{cite web|url=https://news.artnet.com/art-world/hanksy-donald-trump-shit-329090|title=Hanksy Painted a Mural of Donald Trump as a Pile of Poop|last=Boucher|first=Brian|date=31 August 2015|work=Artnet}}</ref>

{{Orphan|date=August 2015}}

'''Hanksy'''  is an anonymous street artist and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist Banky’s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor Tom Hanks.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy’s work has been discussed in numerous publications including: The New York Times, Rolling Stone Magazine, Los Angeles Magazine and the Washington Post and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation Surplus Candy aired on Ovation TV.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> 

== '''Street Art''' ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in Manhattan when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy’s rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on social media, encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>  Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref>  Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his twitter account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== '''Donald Trump''' ===
Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of political commentary when Donald Trump announced his intention to run for President of the United States.[[Hanksy#cite note-3|<sup>[3]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-4|<sup>[4]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-5|<sup>[5]</sup>]] The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by the Huffington Post, depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.[[Hanksy#cite note-huffpo-8|<sup>[8]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-dailynews-9|<sup>[9]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-gothamist-10|<sup>[10]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-cbs-11|<sup>[11]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-artnet-12|<sup>[12]</sup>]]

== '''Installations''' ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== '''Best of the Worst (2014)''' ===
In 2014, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote. <nowiki>http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1</nowiki> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photobooth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''Willy Wonk and the Chocolate Factory,'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former Chase Bank on the Lower East Side.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== '''Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing)''' ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including New York City and Los Angeles. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic West Adams district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== '''Documentaries''' ==

=== '''Ovation TV, “Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy.”''' ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy’s otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called “Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy.”[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]]

'''Hanksy'''  is an anonymous street artist and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist Banky’s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor Tom Hanks.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy’s work has been discussed in numerous publications including: The New York Times, Rolling Stone Magazine, Los Angeles Magazine and the Washington Post and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation Surplus Candy aired on Ovation TV.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> 

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in Manhattan when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy’s rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on social media, encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>  Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref>  Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his twitter account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== '''Donald Trump''' ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of political commentary when Donald Trump announced his intention to run for President of the United States.[[Hanksy#cite note-3|<sup>[3]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-4|<sup>[4]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-5|<sup>[5]</sup>]] The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by the Huffington Post, depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.[[Hanksy#cite note-huffpo-8|<sup>[8]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-dailynews-9|<sup>[9]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-gothamist-10|<sup>[10]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-cbs-11|<sup>[11]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-artnet-12|<sup>[12]</sup>]]

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== '''Best of the Worst (2014)''' ===
In 2014, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote. <nowiki>http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1</nowiki> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photobooth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''Willy Wonk and the Chocolate Factory,'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former Chase Bank on the Lower East Side.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== '''Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing)''' ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including New York City and Los Angeles. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic West Adams district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== '''Ovation TV, “Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy.”''' ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy’s otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called “Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy.”

Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in Manhattan when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy’s rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on social media, encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>  Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref>  Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his twitter account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of political commentary when Donald Trump announced his intention to run for President of the United States.[[Hanksy#cite note-3|<sup>[3]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-4|<sup>[4]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-5|<sup>[5]</sup>]] The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by the Huffington Post, depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.[[Hanksy#cite note-huffpo-8|<sup>[8]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-dailynews-9|<sup>[9]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-gothamist-10|<sup>[10]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-cbs-11|<sup>[11]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-artnet-12|<sup>[12]</sup>]]

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Best of the Worst (2014) ===
In 2014, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote. <nowiki>http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1</nowiki> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photobooth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''Willy Wonk and the Chocolate Factory,'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former Chase Bank on the Lower East Side.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including New York City and Los Angeles. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic West Adams district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy’s otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called “Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy.”

Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in Manhattan when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy’s rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on social media, encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>  Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref>  Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his twitter account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of political commentary when Donald Trump announced his intention to run for President of the United States.[[Hanksy#cite note-3|<sup>[3]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-4|<sup>[4]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-5|<sup>[5]</sup>]] The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by the Huffington Post, depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.[[Hanksy#cite note-huffpo-8|<sup>[8]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-dailynews-9|<sup>[9]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-gothamist-10|<sup>[10]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-cbs-11|<sup>[11]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-artnet-12|<sup>[12]</sup>]]

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Best of the Worst (2014) ===
In 2014, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photobooth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''Willy Wonk and the Chocolate Factory,'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former Chase Bank on the Lower East Side.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including New York City and Los Angeles. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic West Adams district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy’s otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called “Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy.”

Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in Manhattan when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy’s rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on social media, encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>  Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref>  Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his twitter account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of political commentary when Donald Trump announced his intention to run for President of the United States.[[Hanksy#cite note-3|<sup>[3]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-4|<sup>[4]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-5|<sup>[5]</sup>]] The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by the Huffington Post, depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.[[Hanksy#cite note-huffpo-8|<sup>[8]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-dailynews-9|<sup>[9]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-gothamist-10|<sup>[10]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-cbs-11|<sup>[11]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-artnet-12|<sup>[12]</sup>]]

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Best of the Worst (2014) ===
In 2014, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former Chase Bank on the [[Lower East Side]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including New York City and Los Angeles. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic West Adams district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in Manhattan when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy’s rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on social media, encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>  Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref>  Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his twitter account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of political commentary when Donald Trump announced his intention to run for President of the United States.[[Hanksy#cite note-3|<sup>[3]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-4|<sup>[4]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-5|<sup>[5]</sup>]] The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by the Huffington Post, depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.[[Hanksy#cite note-huffpo-8|<sup>[8]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-dailynews-9|<sup>[9]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-gothamist-10|<sup>[10]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-cbs-11|<sup>[11]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-artnet-12|<sup>[12]</sup>]]

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Best of the Worst (2014) ===
In 2014, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former Chase Bank on the [[Lower East Side]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including New York City and Los Angeles. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic West Adams district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy’s rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>  Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref>  Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].[[Hanksy#cite note-3|<sup>[3]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-4|<sup>[4]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-5|<sup>[5]</sup>]] The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by [[The Huffington Post|''the Huffington Post'']], depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.[[Hanksy#cite note-huffpo-8|<sup>[8]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-dailynews-9|<sup>[9]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-gothamist-10|<sup>[10]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-cbs-11|<sup>[11]</sup>]][[Hanksy#cite note-artnet-12|<sup>[12]</sup>]]

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Best of the Worst (2014) ===
In 2014, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

'''Hanksy'''  is an [[Anonymity|anonymous]] [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy’s work has been discussed in numerous publications including: [[The New York Times|''The New York Times'']], [[Rolling Stone|''Rolling Stone'']], [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and [[The Washington Post|''The Washington Post'']] and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation]] TV.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> 

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>  Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref>  Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by [[The Huffington Post|''the Huffington Post'']], depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Best of the Worst (2014) ===
In 2014, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php#photo-1|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

'''Hanksy'''  is an [[Anonymity|anonymous]] [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: [[The New York Times|''The New York Times'']], [[Rolling Stone|''Rolling Stone'']], [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and [[The Washington Post|''The Washington Post'']] and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> 

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by [[The Huffington Post|''the Huffington Post'']], depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Best of the Worst (2014) ===
In 2014, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>
