'''Hanksy'''  is an [[Anonymity|anonymous]] [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com"/> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Best of the Worst (2015) ===
In 2014, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

'''Hanksy'''  is an [[Anonymity|anonymous]] [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com"/> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicts the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Best of the Worst (2015) ===
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" /> 

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> <ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for ‘Best of the Worst’|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

'''Hanksy'''  is an [[Anonymity|anonymous]] [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com"/> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Best of the Worst (2015) ===
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" /> 

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. An homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> <ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for ‘Best of the Worst’|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

'''Hanksy'''  is an [[Anonymity|anonymous]] [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=http://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
[[File:Tronald Dump.jpg|thumb|2015 mural in [[Manhattan]]'s [[Lower East Side]]]] Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com"/> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Best of the Worst (2015) ===
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" /> 

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> <ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for ‘Best of the Worst’|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

'''Hanksy'''  is an [[Anonymity|anonymous]] [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com"/> We don't blame him for going after Trump, since he isn't quite all there. The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Best of the Worst (2015) ===
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" /> 

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> <ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for ‘Best of the Worst’|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

{{Infobox artist
| name = Hanksy

| nationality = American

| movement = graffiti, street art

'''Hanksy'''  is an [[Anonymity|anonymous]] [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archivedate=2016-12-20|df=}}</ref>

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com"/> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archivedate=2017-09-13|df=}}</ref>

=== Best of the Worst (2015) ===
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archivedate=2015-09-21|df=}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" /> 

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archivedate=2015-05-09|df=}}</ref> <ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for ‘Best of the Worst’|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archivedate=2015-09-21|df=}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" /> 

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archivedate=2015-05-09|df=}}</ref> <ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for ‘Best of the Worst’|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

'''Hanksy'''  is the pseudonym for [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] Adam Lucas based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archivedate=2016-12-20|df=}}</ref>

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com"/> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archivedate=2017-09-13|df=}}</ref>

The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archivedate=2015-05-09|df=}}</ref> <ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for ‘Best of the Worst’|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com"/> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archivedate=2017-09-13|df=}}</ref>

=== Best of the Worst (2015) ===
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archivedate=2015-09-21|df=}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archivedate=2015-05-09|df=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for ‘Best of the Worst’|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

'''Hanksy'''  is the pseudonym for [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] Adam Lucas based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|deadurl=yes|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archivedate=2016-12-20|df=}}</ref>

'''Hanksy'''  is the pseudonym for [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] '''Adam Lucas''' based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE ‘HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY’ ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20|df=}}</ref>

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com"/> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last=Writer|first=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13|df=}}</ref>

=== Best of the Worst (2015) ===
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21|df=}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09|df=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for ‘Best of the Worst’|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

== Documentaries ==

=== Ovation TV, "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy." ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's otherwise temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."
