'''Hanksy''' is the pseudonym for [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] '''Adam Lucas''' based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> As the moniker implies, he is best recognized for making humorous, pun-themed work related to popular culture icons and putting those images on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of fellow underground artist [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref>

== Street Art ==
Hanksy started making art in this street format in 2011 in [[Manhattan]] when he created a combination image of the body of Banksy's rat holding a paint roller with the cartoon head of actor Tom Hanks. Rather than paint the work on a traditional canvas, he pasted it on the side of a building in [[Little Italy, Manhattan|Little Italy]] hoping to amuse his friends.<ref name=":1" /> The image soon went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop his street moniker and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web|url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp|title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine|website=www.artnet.com|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=[[The New York Times]]|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2015-08-29}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531|title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday|newspaper=NY Daily News|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

=== Donald Trump ===
Although the majority of his work illustrates lighthearted images that poke fun at celebrities, Hanksy branched out in 2015 to include themes of [[Political criticism|political commentary]] when [[Donald Trump]] announced his intention to run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com"/> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb|title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump|last1=Writer|first1=Priscilla Frank Arts|last2=Post|first2=The Huffington|date=2016-02-26|website=The Huffington Post|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2|title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it|newspaper=Business Insider|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html|title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement'|date=2016-03-27|newspaper=The Independent|language=en-GB|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Installations ==
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

=== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ===
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

=== Best of the Worst (2015) ===
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

'''Hanksy''' is the pseudonym for [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] '''Adam Lucas,''' who is based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Beginning in 2022, Lucas has been working under the pseudonym Adam Himebauch.

== Work ==
He is recognized for painting pun-themed work related to popular culture on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Career ==
Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref>

== Career ==
Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

'''Hanksy''' is the pseudonym for [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] '''Adam Lucas,''' who is based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> Beginning in 2022, Lucas has been working under his actual name '''Adam Lucas Himebauch''' and has advertised a documentary film chroniciling his artistic origins.

== Work ==
He is recognized for painting pun-themed work related to popular culture on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Work ==
Lucas is recognized for painting pun-themed work related to popular culture on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

[[File:Donald Trump (24267946943).jpg|thumb|"Tronald Dump"]]
In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Career ==
Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16}}</ref>

To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

*:
{{Short description|New York City street artist}}
{{Infobox artist
| name             = Hanksy
| image            = Angels and Demons street art by Hanksy.jpg
| caption          = 2012 "Angels and Demons" street art by Hanksy, based on Banksy's 2002 "[[Pulp Fiction (Banksy)|Pulp Fiction]]"
| nationality      = American
| known_for        = [[street art]]
| movement         = 
| website          = 
}}

'''Hanksy''' is the pseudonym for [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] '''Adam Lucas Himebauch''', who is based in [[New York City]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> His artwork includes recreations of [[Banksy]] street art adapted to include the face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].

Beginning in 2022, Lucas has been working under his actual name and has advertised a documentary film chronicling his artistic origins.

== Work ==
Lucas is recognized for painting pun-themed work related to popular culture on the street; for instance, in the work that inspired his artist name, Hanksy combined the body of [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Since 2011, Hanksy has created hundreds of images that he continues to paste on the streets but also paint on canvases that are exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

[[File:Donald Trump (24267946943).jpg|thumb|Hanksy's depiction of Donald Trump]]
In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Career ==
Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|last=Perler|first=Elie|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

== Work as Hanksy ==
As Hanksy, Himebauch is recognized for painting pun-themed work related to popular culture on the street; for instance, combining the body of [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]]. The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Between 2011 and 2019, Hanksy created hundreds of images that he pasted on the streets and painted on canvases that were exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Work as Adam Lucas Himebauch ==

=== ''Back to the Future'', 2022 ===
In 2022, Himebauch created an immersive conceptual performance, fabricating the retrospective of an iconic artist from the 1970s and 1980s who happened to be himself aged by decades.<ref name=":3" /> Using faux archival footage, grainy photographs and advertisements for exhibitions that never took place and textbooks that were never written, Himebauch tested the boundaries of expectations and reality, of past and present and art and artifice.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Scholl |first=Clara |last2=Arafeh |first2=Alia |date=2022-12-12 |title=The unexpected connection between Adam Himebauch, Adam Lucas and Hanksy |url=https://nyunews.com/arts/2022/12/12/adam-himebauch-lucas-hanksy/ |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Washington Square News}}</ref>

The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|last=Perler|first=Elie|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

== Work as Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch ==
Working under the names Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch rather than his street art moniker, Himebauch maintains his signature whimsy, humor and interest in conceptualism and performance.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-09-27 |title=Who is Adam Himebauch? |url=https://hypebeast.com/2022/9/before-and-back-adam-himebauch-documentary |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> In 2020, the artist donated two paintings to a benefit auction held by Future Galerie to help raise money for SkyArt a free and accessible art center serving underprivileged youth in Chicago.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-07-10 |title=Adam Lucas, Max Sansing and More Artists Donate Works to Support Social Justice Organizations |url=https://hypebeast.com/2020/7/future-galerie-online-art-charity-auction-social-justice-organizations |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> 

=== ''Back to the Future'', 2022 ===
In 2022, Himebauch created an immersive conceptual performance, fabricating the retrospective of an iconic artist from the 1970s and 1980s who happened to be himself aged by decades.<ref name=":3" /> Using faux archival footage, grainy photographs and advertisements for exhibitions that never took place and textbooks that were never written, Himebauch tested the boundaries of expectations and reality, of past and present and art and artifice.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Scholl |first=Clara |last2=Arafeh |first2=Alia |date=2022-12-12 |title=The unexpected connection between Adam Himebauch, Adam Lucas and Hanksy |url=https://nyunews.com/arts/2022/12/12/adam-himebauch-lucas-hanksy/ |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Washington Square News}}</ref>

'''Hanksy''' is the pseudonym for New York City-based [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] '''Adam Lucas''', who also goes by '''Adam Himebauch'''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> His artwork under the moniker Hanksy includes recreations of [[Banksy]] street art adapted to include a cartoon interpretation of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

Beginning in 2022, Himebauch began working under his actual name and has advertised a documentary film chronicling his artistic origins.<ref name=":3">{{Cite news |last=Sherman |first=Rachel |date=2022-12-14 |title=Artist or Artifice: Who Is Adam Himebauch? |language=en-US |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/14/arts/design/con-artist-adam-himebauch.html |access-date=2023-06-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref>

== Work as Hanksy ==
As Hanksy, Himebauch is recognized for painting pun-themed work related to popular culture on the street; for instance, combining the body of [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]]. The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Between 2011 and 2017, Hanksy created hundreds of images that he pasted on the streets and painted on canvases that were exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Hanksy's Career ==
Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref> In 2021, three years after retiring his Hanksy persona, the artist released a collection of Hanksy [[Non-fungible token|NFT]]'s on [[OpenSea]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-09 |title=Hanksy Is Invading the Crypto World With One Silly NFT at a Time |url=https://hypebeast.com/2021/4/adam-lucas-hanksy-opens-collection-nft-interview |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|last=Perler|first=Elie|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

{{Short description|New York City street artist}}
{{Infobox artist
| name             = Hanksy
| image            = Angels and Demons street art by Hanksy.jpg
| caption          = 2012 "Angels and Demons" street art by Hanksy, based on Banksy's 2002 "[[Pulp Fiction (Banksy)|Pulp Fiction]]"
| nationality      = American
| known_for        = [[street art]]
| movement         = 
| website          = 
}}

'''Hanksy''' is the pseudonym for New York City-based [[street artist]] and [[parodist]] '''Adam Lucas''', who also goes by '''Adam Himebauch'''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> His artwork under the moniker Hanksy includes recreations of [[Banksy]] street art adapted to include a cartoon interpretation of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

Beginning in 2022, Himebauch began working under his actual name and has advertised a documentary film chronicling his artistic origins.<ref name=":3">{{Cite news |last=Sherman |first=Rachel |date=2022-12-14 |title=Artist or Artifice: Who Is Adam Himebauch? |language=en-US |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/14/arts/design/con-artist-adam-himebauch.html |access-date=2023-06-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref>

== Work as Hanksy ==
As Hanksy, Himebauch is recognized for painting pun-themed work related to popular culture on the street; for instance, combining the body of [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]]. The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Between 2011 and 2017, Hanksy created hundreds of images that he pasted on the streets and painted on canvases that were exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Hanksy's Career ==
Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref> In 2021, three years after retiring his Hanksy persona, the artist released a collection of Hanksy [[Non-fungible token|NFT]]'s on [[OpenSea]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-09 |title=Hanksy Is Invading the Crypto World With One Silly NFT at a Time |url=https://hypebeast.com/2021/4/adam-lucas-hanksy-opens-collection-nft-interview |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|last=Perler|first=Elie|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

== Work as Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch ==
Working under the names Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch rather than his street art moniker, Himebauch maintains his signature whimsy, humor and interest in conceptualism and performance.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-09-27 |title=Who is Adam Himebauch? |url=https://hypebeast.com/2022/9/before-and-back-adam-himebauch-documentary |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> In 2020, the artist donated two paintings to a benefit auction held by Future Galerie to help raise money for SkyArt a free and accessible art center serving underprivileged youth in Chicago.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-07-10 |title=Adam Lucas, Max Sansing and More Artists Donate Works to Support Social Justice Organizations |url=https://hypebeast.com/2020/7/future-galerie-online-art-charity-auction-social-justice-organizations |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> 

=== ''Back to the Future'', 2022 ===
In 2022, Himebauch created an immersive conceptual performance, fabricating the retrospective of an iconic artist from the 1970s and 1980s who happened to be himself aged by decades.<ref name=":3" /> Using faux archival footage, grainy photographs and advertisements for exhibitions that never took place and textbooks that were never written, Himebauch tested the boundaries of expectations and reality, of past and present and art and artifice.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Scholl |first=Clara |last2=Arafeh |first2=Alia |date=2022-12-12 |title=The unexpected connection between Adam Himebauch, Adam Lucas and Hanksy |url=https://nyunews.com/arts/2022/12/12/adam-himebauch-lucas-hanksy/ |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Washington Square News}}</ref>
