As Hanksy, Himebauch is recognized for painting pun-themed work related to popular culture on the street; for instance, combining the body of [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]]. The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Between 2011 and 2017, Hanksy created hundreds of images that he pasted on the streets and painted on canvases that were exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Hanksy's Career ==
Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref> In 2021, three years after retiring his Hanksy persona, the artist released a collection of Hanksy [[Non-fungible token|NFT]]'s on [[OpenSea]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-09 |title=Hanksy Is Invading the Crypto World With One Silly NFT at a Time |url=https://hypebeast.com/2021/4/adam-lucas-hanksy-opens-collection-nft-interview |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.[[Hanksy#cite note-6|<sup>[6]</sup>]] In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|last=Perler|first=Elie|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

== Work as Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch ==
Working under the names Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch rather than his street art moniker, Himebauch maintains his signature whimsy, humor and interest in conceptualism and performance.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-09-27 |title=Who is Adam Himebauch? |url=https://hypebeast.com/2022/9/before-and-back-adam-himebauch-documentary |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> In 2020, the artist donated two paintings to a benefit auction held by Future Galerie to help raise money for SkyArt a free and accessible art center serving underprivileged youth in Chicago.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-07-10 |title=Adam Lucas, Max Sansing and More Artists Donate Works to Support Social Justice Organizations |url=https://hypebeast.com/2020/7/future-galerie-online-art-charity-auction-social-justice-organizations |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> 

=== ''Back to the Future'', 2022 ===
In 2022, Himebauch created an immersive conceptual performance, fabricating the retrospective of an iconic artist from the 1970s and 1980s who happened to be himself aged by decades.<ref name=":3" /> Using faux archival footage, grainy photographs and advertisements for exhibitions that never took place and textbooks that were never written, Himebauch tested the boundaries of expectations and reality, of past and present and art and artifice.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Scholl |first=Clara |last2=Arafeh |first2=Alia |date=2022-12-12 |title=The unexpected connection between Adam Himebauch, Adam Lucas and Hanksy |url=https://nyunews.com/arts/2022/12/12/adam-himebauch-lucas-hanksy/ |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Washington Square News}}</ref>

In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Hanksy's Career ==
Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref> In 2021, three years after retiring his Hanksy persona, the artist released a collection of Hanksy [[Non-fungible token|NFT]]'s on [[OpenSea]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-09 |title=Hanksy Is Invading the Crypto World With One Silly NFT at a Time |url=https://hypebeast.com/2021/4/adam-lucas-hanksy-opens-collection-nft-interview |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}} {{verify source |date=September 2023 |reason=This ref was deleted Special:Diff/1163557612 by a bug in VisualEditor and later restored by a bot from the original cite located at Special:Permalink/1163557557 cite #6 - verify the cite is accurate and delete this template. [[User:GreenC_bot/Job_18]]}}</ref> In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|last=Perler|first=Elie|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

== Work as Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch ==
Working under the names Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch rather than his street art moniker, Himebauch maintains his signature whimsy, humor and interest in conceptualism and performance.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-09-27 |title=Who is Adam Himebauch? |url=https://hypebeast.com/2022/9/before-and-back-adam-himebauch-documentary |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> In 2020, the artist donated two paintings to a benefit auction held by Future Galerie to help raise money for SkyArt a free and accessible art center serving underprivileged youth in Chicago.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-07-10 |title=Adam Lucas, Max Sansing and More Artists Donate Works to Support Social Justice Organizations |url=https://hypebeast.com/2020/7/future-galerie-online-art-charity-auction-social-justice-organizations |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== ''Back to the Future'', 2022 ===
In 2022, Himebauch created an immersive conceptual performance, fabricating the retrospective of an iconic artist from the 1970s and 1980s who happened to be himself aged by decades.<ref name=":3" /> Using faux archival footage, grainy photographs and advertisements for exhibitions that never took place and textbooks that were never written, Himebauch tested the boundaries of expectations and reality, of past and present and art and artifice.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Scholl |first=Clara |last2=Arafeh |first2=Alia |date=2022-12-12 |title=The unexpected connection between Adam Himebauch, Adam Lucas and Hanksy |url=https://nyunews.com/arts/2022/12/12/adam-himebauch-lucas-hanksy/ |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Washington Square News}}</ref>

In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Hanksy's career ==
Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref> In 2021, three years after retiring his Hanksy persona, the artist released a collection of Hanksy [[Non-fungible token|NFT]]'s on [[OpenSea]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-09 |title=Hanksy Is Invading the Crypto World With One Silly NFT at a Time |url=https://hypebeast.com/2021/4/adam-lucas-hanksy-opens-collection-nft-interview |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}} {{verify source |date=September 2023 |reason=This ref was deleted Special:Diff/1163557612 by a bug in VisualEditor and later restored by a bot from the original cite located at Special:Permalink/1163557557 cite #6 - verify the cite is accurate and delete this template. [[User:GreenC_bot/Job_18]]}}</ref> In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|last=Perler|first=Elie|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

== Work as Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch ==
Working under the names Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch rather than his street art moniker, Himebauch maintains his signature whimsy, humor and interest in conceptualism and performance.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-09-27 |title=Who is Adam Himebauch? |url=https://hypebeast.com/2022/9/before-and-back-adam-himebauch-documentary |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> In 2020, the artist donated two paintings to a benefit auction held by Future Galerie to help raise money for SkyArt a free and accessible art center serving underprivileged youth in Chicago.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-07-10 |title=Adam Lucas, Max Sansing and More Artists Donate Works to Support Social Justice Organizations |url=https://hypebeast.com/2020/7/future-galerie-online-art-charity-auction-social-justice-organizations |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== ''Back to the Future'', 2022 ===
In 2022, Himebauch created an immersive conceptual performance, fabricating the retrospective of an iconic artist from the 1970s and 1980s who happened to be himself aged by decades.<ref name=":3" /> Using faux archival footage, grainy photographs and advertisements for exhibitions that never took place and textbooks that were never written, Himebauch tested the boundaries of expectations and reality, of past and present and art and artifice.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Scholl |first=Clara |last2=Arafeh |first2=Alia |date=2022-12-12 |title=The unexpected connection between Adam Himebauch, Adam Lucas and Hanksy |url=https://nyunews.com/arts/2022/12/12/adam-himebauch-lucas-hanksy/ |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Washington Square News}}</ref>

The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Between 2011 and 2017, Hanksy created hundreds of images that he pasted on the streets and painted on canvases that were exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

== Hanksy's career ==
Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref> In 2021, three years after retiring his Hanksy persona, the artist released a collection of Hanksy [[Non-fungible token|NFT]]'s on [[OpenSea]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-09 |title=Hanksy Is Invading the Crypto World With One Silly NFT at a Time |url=https://hypebeast.com/2021/4/adam-lucas-hanksy-opens-collection-nft-interview |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}} {{verify source |date=September 2023 |reason=This ref was deleted Special:Diff/1163557612 by a bug in VisualEditor and later restored by a bot from the original cite located at Special:Permalink/1163557557 cite #6 - verify the cite is accurate and delete this template. [[User:GreenC_bot/Job_18]]}}</ref> In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|last=Perler|first=Elie|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

== Work as Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch ==
Working under the names Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch rather than his street art moniker, Himebauch maintains his signature whimsy, humor and interest in conceptualism and performance.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-09-27 |title=Who is Adam Himebauch? |url=https://hypebeast.com/2022/9/before-and-back-adam-himebauch-documentary |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> In 2020, the artist donated two paintings to a benefit auction held by Future Galerie to help raise money for SkyArt a free and accessible art center serving underprivileged youth in Chicago.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-07-10 |title=Adam Lucas, Max Sansing and More Artists Donate Works to Support Social Justice Organizations |url=https://hypebeast.com/2020/7/future-galerie-online-art-charity-auction-social-justice-organizations |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== ''Back to the Future'', 2022 ===
In 2022, Himebauch created an immersive conceptual performance, fabricating the retrospective of an iconic artist from the 1970s and 1980s who happened to be himself aged by decades.<ref name=":3" /> Using faux archival footage, grainy photographs and advertisements for exhibitions that never took place and textbooks that were never written, Himebauch tested the boundaries of expectations and reality, of past and present and art and artifice.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Scholl |first=Clara |last2=Arafeh |first2=Alia |date=2022-12-12 |title=The unexpected connection between Adam Himebauch, Adam Lucas and Hanksy |url=https://nyunews.com/arts/2022/12/12/adam-himebauch-lucas-hanksy/ |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Washington Square News}}</ref>

== Work as Hanksy ==
As Hanksy, Himebauch is recognized for painting pun-themed work related to popular culture on the street; for instance, combining the body of [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]]. The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Between 2011 and 2017, Hanksy created hundreds of images that he pasted on the streets and painted on canvases that were exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=OVATION TO PREMIERE 'HANKSY PRESENTS: SURPLUS CANDY' ACROSS LINEAR, DIGITAL & SOCIAL PLATFORMS ON JUNE 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV {{!}} Ovation Official Site|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref> In 2021, three years after retiring his Hanksy persona, the artist released a collection of Hanksy [[Non-fungible token|NFT]]'s on [[OpenSea]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-09 |title=Hanksy Is Invading the Crypto World With One Silly NFT at a Time |url=https://hypebeast.com/2021/4/adam-lucas-hanksy-opens-collection-nft-interview |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}} {{verify source |date=September 2023 |reason=This ref was deleted Special:Diff/1163557612 by a bug in VisualEditor and later restored by a bot from the original cite located at Special:Permalink/1163557557 cite #6 - verify the cite is accurate and delete this template. [[User:GreenC_bot/Job_18]]}}</ref> In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|last=Perler|first=Elie|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

== Work as Adam Himebauch and Adam Lucas ==
Working under the names Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch rather than his street art moniker, Himebauch maintains his signature whimsy, humor and interest in [[conceptual art]] and performance.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-09-27 |title=Who is Adam Himebauch? |url=https://hypebeast.com/2022/9/before-and-back-adam-himebauch-documentary |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> 

=== ''Back to the Future'', 2022 ===
In 2022, Himebauch created an immersive conceptual performance, fabricating the retrospective of an iconic artist from the 1970s and 1980s who happened to be himself aged by decades.<ref name=":3" /> Using faux archival footage, grainy photographs and advertisements for exhibitions that never took place and textbooks that were never written, Himebauch tested the boundaries of expectations and reality, of past and present and art and artifice.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Scholl |first=Clara |last2=Arafeh |first2=Alia |date=2022-12-12 |title=The unexpected connection between Adam Himebauch, Adam Lucas and Hanksy |url=https://nyunews.com/arts/2022/12/12/adam-himebauch-lucas-hanksy/ |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Washington Square News}}</ref>

=== '''''Never Ever Land'', 2024''' ===
In 2024, Himebauch staged a month-long performance in which he meditated on a white slab with a pillow at the Ceysson & Bénétière Gallery in New York for the entire duration of the project.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Adam Himebauch 2024-02-08 - Ceysson & Bénétière |url=https://www.ceyssonbenetiere.com/en/exhibitions/1280/adam-himebauch-new-york-2024-1280 |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=www.ceyssonbenetiere.com}}</ref> The performance was [[Live streaming|livestreamed]] 24 hours a day on [[YouTube]] and was supposedly about mortality, sacrifice and challenging bodily limits.<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |date=2024-02-12 |title=Is Adam Himebauch Really Staging a Sleep-In at a New York Gallery? |url=https://news.artnet.com/art-world/adam-himebauch-never-ever-land-sleep-in-2432144 |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=Artnet News |language=en-US}}</ref> The livestream was actually only 2 hours’ worth of filming that was played continuously on a loop, giving the impression that Himebauch was meditating at the gallery when he was not. The ultimate purpose of this performance was to encourage viewers to question their expectations of art, and confront the realities that media can be an artifice.<ref name=":4" /> Himebauch also exhibited collages and large-scale paintings of landscapes, painted with window frames to simulate the experience of looking out of a window.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Adam Himebauch: Never Ever Land {{!}} Exhibitions {{!}} MutualArt |url=https://www.mutualart.com/Exhibition/Adam-Himebauch--Never-Ever-Land/DC3C2754FEB179C3 |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=www.mutualart.com |language=en}}</ref>

=== '''''Here Comes the Twister'', 2024''' ===
Held at Gana Art Nineone in [[Korea]], this exhibition was a reaction to feedback Himebauch had received from critics to “just paint.”<ref>{{Cite web |title=ADAM HIMEBAUCH |url=https://www.ganaart.com/exhibition/adam-himebauch/ |access-date=2025-10-04 |language=ko}}</ref> The show was comprised of paintings that collectively chronicle the evolution of a tornado; however, the opening reception was also a performance piece.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Adam Himebauch: Here comes the... {{!}} Exhibitions {{!}} MutualArt |url=https://www.mutualart.com/Exhibition/Adam-Himebauch--Here-comes-the-twister/42F77B5117F28CFD |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=www.mutualart.com |language=en}}</ref> Viewers entered the gallery expecting to see art, and were met with empty walls, until Himebauch entered the space and stappled loose-canvas paintings directly onto the walls himself. After all of the paintings were stapled to the walls and unfurled from their tubes, Himebauch danced to the song "Fantastic Man” by [[William Onyeabor]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-06-25 |title=Adam Himebach Turns the Installation of His Seoul Show Into Performance |url=https://news.artnet.com/art-world/adam-himebauch-hangs-his-own-show-seoul-2503747 |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=Artnet News |language=en-US}}</ref>

=== Select Exhibitions ===
In 2025, Himebauch had a solo exhibition entitled ''Serenity Now'' at Jupiter Miami where he showed a series of [[Monochrome|monochromatic]] landscapes inspired by pre-urban Miami.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Adam Himebauch - Overview |url=https://jupiterarms.com/exhibitions/34-adam-himebauch-serenity-now/overview/ |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=Jupiter |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-02-03 |title=Adam Himebauch Trades Stunts for Stillness in ‘Serenity Now’ |url=https://hypebeast.com/2025/2/adam-himebauch-serenity-now-jupiter-miami |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> This series focused on restrained reflection and painting alone rather than on provocative performance.<ref>{{Cite web |last=James |first=Jesse |date=2025-02-04 |title=Jupiter Miami Presents Serenity Now: A Solo Exhibition by Adam Himebauch |url=https://stupiddope.com/2025/02/jupiter-miami-presents-serenity-now-a-solo-exhibition-by-adam-himebauch/ |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=stupidDOPE {{!}} Est. 2008 |language=en-US}}</ref>

'''Adam Himebauch'''  is a contemporary painter, performance and [[Installation art|installation artist]]. Himebauch began his career as a New York City street artist and parodist using the pseudonym '''Hanksy'''.<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/videos/meet-hanksy-the-pun-loving-graffiti-trickster-with-a-serious-cult-following-20150331|title=Meet Hanksy, the Masked Graffiti Trickster Who Loves to Pun|newspaper=Rolling Stone|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref> His artwork under the moniker Hanksy includes recreations of [[Banksy]] street art adapted to include a cartoon interpretation of actor [[Tom Hanks]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html|title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy|last=Leland|first=John|date=2014-02-14|newspaper=The New York Times|issn=0362-4331|access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

Beginning in 2022, Himebauch began working under his actual name and has advertised a documentary film chronicling his artistic origins.<ref name=":3">{{Cite news |last=Sherman |first=Rachel |date=2022-12-14 |title=Artist or Artifice: Who Is Adam Himebauch? |language=en-US |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/12/14/arts/design/con-artist-adam-himebauch.html |access-date=2023-06-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref>

== Work as Hanksy ==
As Hanksy, Himebauch is recognized for painting pun-themed work related to popular culture on the street; for instance, combining the body of [[Banksy]]'s classic rat with a cartoon face of actor [[Tom Hanks]]. The image went viral on [[social media]], encouraging Hanksy to develop and expand his portfolio of popular culture mashup puns.<ref name="artnet.com">{{Cite web |title=Reverend Jen exclusive on Hanksy, the Tom Hanks-Banksy mashup artist - artnet Magazine |url=http://www.artnet.com/magazineus/features/jen/reverend-jen-hanksy-art-show-1-17-12.asp |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=www.artnet.com}}</ref> Between 2011 and 2017, Hanksy created hundreds of images that he pasted on the streets and painted on canvases that were exhibited across the country.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Leland |first=John |date=2014-02-14 |title=A Parodist Who Calls Himself Hanksy |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2014/02/16/nyregion/a-parodist-who-calls-himself-hanksy.html |access-date=2015-08-29 |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Banksy has yet to comment on the parody; however, Tom Hanks is supportive of the work and shared a photograph posing next to Hanksy on his [[Twitter]] account.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

In 2015 he made work critical of [[Donald Trump]]'s run for [[President of the United States]].<ref name="artnet.com" /> The image he created in protest, dubbed ''Tronald Dump'' by ''[[the Huffington Post]]'', depicted the then candidate as a cartoonish pile of feces with flies encircling his head.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Writer |first1=Priscilla Frank Arts |last2=Post |first2=The Huffington |date=2016-02-26 |title='Dump Across America': A Street Art Project Devoted To Taking Down Trump |url=http://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/donald-trump-is-a-steaming-pile-of-sht-according-to-street-artists-campaign_us_56d08be8e4b0bf0dab31e7cb |access-date=2016-12-14 |website=The Huffington Post}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=The street artist who painted Donald Trump as a poop emoji tells us why he did it |newspaper=Business Insider |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/why-a-street-artist-is-trolling-trump-2016-2 |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2016-03-27 |title='Dump Trump': Artist Hanksy on a 'grassroots (bowel) movement' |language=en-GB |newspaper=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-elections/dump-donald-trump-artist-hanksy-on-the-grassroots-bowel-movement-that-is-defining-the-2016-us-a6955676.html |access-date=2016-12-14}}</ref>

Hanksy's work has been discussed in numerous publications including: ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[Rolling Stone]]'', [[Los Angeles (magazine)|''Los Angeles Magazine'']] and ''[[The Washington Post]]'' and in 2016, a documentary series related to his installation ''Surplus Candy'' aired on [[Ovation (U.S. TV channel)|Ovation TV]].<ref name="ovationtv.com">{{Cite news|url=http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|title=Ovation to Premiere 'Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy' Across Linear, Digital & Social Platforms on June 26|date=2016-05-24|newspaper=Art, Culture, Film, Music, Fashion, Arts Advocacy TV |language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220204450/http://www.ovationtv.com/ovation-to-premiere-hanksy-presents-surplus-candy-across-linear-digital-social-platforms-on-june-26/|archive-date=2016-12-20}}</ref> In 2021, three years after retiring his Hanksy persona, the artist released a collection of Hanksy [[Non-fungible token|NFT]]'s on [[OpenSea]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-04-09 |title=Hanksy Is Invading the Crypto World With One Silly NFT at a Time |url=https://hypebeast.com/2021/4/adam-lucas-hanksy-opens-collection-nft-interview |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref>

=== Installations ===
In addition to his street and canvas art, Hanksy has created several interactive installations meant to engage his audience in innovative and participatory ways.

==== Surplus Candy (2014-ongoing) ====
Sparked by the success of the ''Best of the Worst'' installation, Hanksy began his ''Surplus Candy'' initiative—highly interactive events that he has hosted in multiple cities including [[New York City]] and [[Los Angeles]]. In each installment, Hanksy secures an abandoned space, transforming it with art and activity, then opens that space up to the public for only a couple of hours before taking it down.<ref name="ovationtv.com" /> In 2014, when he first hosted ''Surplus Candy'' in Manhattan, Hanksy included twenty other artists.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Street artist Hanksy on Tom Hanks' films on the actor's 60th bday |newspaper=NY Daily News |url=http://www.nydailynews.com/entertainment/movies/street-artist-hanksy-tom-hanks-films-actor-60th-bday-article-1.2704531 |access-date=2016-12-14}} {{verify source |date=September 2023 |reason=This ref was deleted Special:Diff/1163557612 by a bug in VisualEditor and later restored by a bot from the original cite located at Special:Permalink/1163557557 cite #6 - verify the cite is accurate and delete this template. [[User:GreenC_bot/Job_18]]}}</ref> In 2015, when he installed the show in a dilapidated mansion in the historic [[West Adams, Los Angeles|West Adams]] district in Los Angeles, over 90 artists were involved.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|title=Street Artist Hanksy Is Taking Over A 'Massive, Historic' Mansion|newspaper=LAist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170913113014/http://laist.com/2015/10/05/street_artist_hanksy_is_taking_over.php|archive-date=2017-09-13}}</ref>

==== Best of the Worst (2015) ====
In 2015, Hanksy mounted an interactive exhibition entitled ''Best of the Worst'' that featured some of his signature pun pieces, including portraits of Bill Murrito, Hamuel L. Jackson, Kanye Brest, Traylor Swift, Pikajew, Drak-o Malfoy and Mile E. Coyote.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|title=Hanksy Brings His "Best Of The Worst" To Old LES Chase Bank|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921063826/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/29/hanksy_best_of_the_worst.php|archive-date=2015-09-21}}</ref> In addition to exhibiting his own work, Hanksy showcased pieces by fellow street artists, built a functional skateboarding ramp, included a photo booth, arcade games, balloon artist and a live-DJ.<ref name=":2" />

To generate awareness of the exhibition and active interest in attending, Hanksy created Golden Tickets that he hid throughout Manhattan, teasing their location on social media. A homage to the 1971 fantasy film, ''[[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory|Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory]],'' these tickets introduced the interactive nature of the event but also could be traded in for a limited edition work by the artist. The exhibition itself took place at a former [[Chase Bank]] on the [[Lower East Side]] that Hanksy transformed to look like a street in [[Chinatown, Manhattan|Chinatown]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|title=We're Hiding Golden Tickets Around NYC With Hanksy|newspaper=Gothamist|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-14|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150509061345/http://gothamist.com/2015/03/18/hanksy_golden_ticket.php|archive-date=2015-05-09}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|title=Recap: Hanksy Transforms Chase Bank for 'Best of the Worst'|last=Perler|first=Elie|date=2015-03-30|newspaper=Bowery Boogie|language=en-US|access-date=2016-12-16|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813125350/https://boweryboogie.com/2015/03/recap-hanksy-transforms-chase-bank-for-best-of-the-worst/|archive-date=2022-08-13}}</ref>

=== TV series ===
To preserve a record of Hanksy's temporary installations, Ovation TV launched a six-episode series called "Hanksy Presents: Surplus Candy."

== Work as Adam Himebauch and Adam Lucas ==
Working under the names Adam Lucas and Adam Himebauch rather than his street art moniker, Himebauch maintains his signature whimsy, humor and interest in [[conceptual art]] and performance.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-09-27 |title=Who is Adam Himebauch? |url=https://hypebeast.com/2022/9/before-and-back-adam-himebauch-documentary |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> 

=== ''Back to the Future'', 2022 ===
In 2022, Himebauch created an immersive conceptual performance, fabricating the retrospective of an iconic artist from the 1970s and 1980s who happened to be himself aged by decades.<ref name=":3" /> Using faux archival footage, grainy photographs and advertisements for exhibitions that never took place and textbooks that were never written, Himebauch tested the boundaries of expectations and reality, of past and present and art and artifice.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Scholl |first=Clara |last2=Arafeh |first2=Alia |date=2022-12-12 |title=The unexpected connection between Adam Himebauch, Adam Lucas and Hanksy |url=https://nyunews.com/arts/2022/12/12/adam-himebauch-lucas-hanksy/ |access-date=2023-06-29 |website=Washington Square News}}</ref>

=== ''Never Ever Land'', 2024 ===
In 2024, Himebauch staged a month-long performance in which he meditated on a white slab with a pillow at the Ceysson & Bénétière Gallery in New York for the entire duration of the project.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Adam Himebauch 2024-02-08 - Ceysson & Bénétière |url=https://www.ceyssonbenetiere.com/en/exhibitions/1280/adam-himebauch-new-york-2024-1280 |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=www.ceyssonbenetiere.com}}</ref> The performance was [[Live streaming|livestreamed]] 24 hours a day on [[YouTube]] and was supposedly about mortality, sacrifice and challenging bodily limits.<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |date=2024-02-12 |title=Is Adam Himebauch Really Staging a Sleep-In at a New York Gallery? |url=https://news.artnet.com/art-world/adam-himebauch-never-ever-land-sleep-in-2432144 |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=Artnet News |language=en-US}}</ref> The livestream was actually only 2 hours’ worth of filming that was played continuously on a loop, giving the impression that Himebauch was meditating at the gallery when he was not. The ultimate purpose of this performance was to encourage viewers to question their expectations of art, and confront the realities that media can be an artifice.<ref name=":4" /> Himebauch also exhibited collages and large-scale paintings of landscapes, painted with window frames to simulate the experience of looking out of a window.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Adam Himebauch: Never Ever Land {{!}} Exhibitions |url=https://www.mutualart.com/Exhibition/Adam-Himebauch--Never-Ever-Land/DC3C2754FEB179C3 |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=www.mutualart.com |language=en}}</ref>

=== ''Here Comes the Twister'', 2024 ===
Held at Gana Art Nineone in [[Korea]], this exhibition was a reaction to feedback Himebauch had received from critics to “just paint.”<ref>{{Cite web |title=Adam Himebauch |url=https://www.ganaart.com/exhibition/adam-himebauch/ |access-date=2025-10-04 |language=ko}}</ref> The show was comprised of paintings that collectively chronicle the evolution of a tornado; however, the opening reception was also a performance piece.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Adam Himebauch: Here comes the... {{!}} Exhibitions |url=https://www.mutualart.com/Exhibition/Adam-Himebauch--Here-comes-the-twister/42F77B5117F28CFD |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=www.mutualart.com |language=en}}</ref> Viewers entered the gallery expecting to see art, and were met with empty walls, until Himebauch entered the space and stappled loose-canvas paintings directly onto the walls himself. After all of the paintings were stapled to the walls and unfurled from their tubes, Himebauch danced to the song "Fantastic Man” by [[William Onyeabor]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-06-25 |title=Adam Himebach Turns the Installation of His Seoul Show Into Performance |url=https://news.artnet.com/art-world/adam-himebauch-hangs-his-own-show-seoul-2503747 |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=Artnet News |language=en-US}}</ref>

=== Select exhibitions ===
In 2025, Himebauch had a solo exhibition entitled ''Serenity Now'' at Jupiter Miami where he showed a series of [[Monochrome|monochromatic]] landscapes inspired by pre-urban Miami.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Adam Himebauch - Overview |url=https://jupiterarms.com/exhibitions/34-adam-himebauch-serenity-now/overview/ |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=Jupiter |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-02-03 |title=Adam Himebauch Trades Stunts for Stillness in ‘Serenity Now’ |url=https://hypebeast.com/2025/2/adam-himebauch-serenity-now-jupiter-miami |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=Hypebeast}}</ref> This series focused on restrained reflection and painting alone rather than on provocative performance.<ref>{{Cite web |last=James |first=Jesse |date=2025-02-04 |title=Jupiter Miami Presents Serenity Now: A Solo Exhibition by Adam Himebauch |url=https://stupiddope.com/2025/02/jupiter-miami-presents-serenity-now-a-solo-exhibition-by-adam-himebauch/ |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=stupidDOPE |language=en-US}}</ref>

{{Infobox artist

| image            = Artist Adam Himebauch in his studio.jpg
